steps.text: Perform a balancing test to determine whether the benefits outweigh the risk of accepting a gift.
Determine if any employee’s ability to carry out responsibilities is influenced by the gift.
Assess if the acceptance of the gift compromises the integrity of any government program.
Evaluate if the donor has an expectation of receiving a future benefit.
Consult under Section 231 of the PHS Act whether the gift is conditioned upon an unmet expenditure.
Review potential undue influence from accepting the gift.
The CIO Gift Review Official is responsible for reviewing non-monetary gifts.
Document the review of gifts including donor and gift description.
Consider the monetary value of the gift and record it.
Consult with OGC if there is a potential conflict of interest concern.
Request a CDC COI Panel Review if further evaluation is needed.
Forward the cash or cash equivalents for deposit to the Deputy Chief Financial Officer within 24 hours of receipt.
Accompany the gift transmittal with a brief memorandum stating that the gift is pre-decisional.
Include any documentation that accompanied the gift.
Mail gift fund checks to the designated addresses.
Transmit wired funds via pay.gov.
Deposit the gift in a suspense account unless it is officially accepted within the 24-hour period.
Return monetary gift checks more than 90 days old to the recipient organization for return to the donor for reissuance.
Determine if the proposed gift has been recommended for approval by the Conflict-of-Interest panel.
Decide whether to accept the gift.
Notify the donor of the determination.
Use the gift acceptance checklists for all gifts when making a decision on the acceptance of a monetary gift.
Submit the checklists through OGC and OFR to the designated approving official for review and approval before acceptance occurs.
Ensure that the gift meets the criteria of the pre-acceptance checklist and that all supporting documentation is complete.
Accept gifts less than $500 without CDC Conflict of Interest Panel Review.
Determine that the donor and gift are an acceptable source.
Obtain written agreement from the potential donor for conditional monetary gifts.
Contact the donor to obtain permission in writing to use any amount remaining exceeding the $1,000 threshold.
Coordinate establishing a CAN for the gift.
Prepare a letter of acceptance from CDC to the donor stating the monetary gift is being deposited.
Deposit the money into the gift fund account if the monetary gift is accepted.
Return any uncashed checks to the donor if the monetary gift is not accepted.
Thank the donor for non-monetary gifts and inform them that the donation will be considered for acceptance.
Notify the Logistics Management Branch of proposals for personal property gifts.
Submit a copy of the document accepting the personal property gift to OSSAM.
Create a basic record in the on-line property system upon notification of acceptance of the gift by the CIO property custodian.
Request in writing that the Chief Financial Officer invest the excess funds in interest-bearing obligations of the United States if there are excess funds of $50,000 or more.
Include information concerning when these investments will be redeemed in order to meet planned outlays.
Prepare the director's memorandum that must include the amount of funds invested, the source of funds, and any recommendations regarding purchase of the U.S. Treasury bills, notes, or bonds.
Return the monies upon liquidation of investments to the same gift fund account to be used for the benefit of the CIOs.

preconditions.text: Gift funds expenditures for entertainment must be pre-approved by OFR and meet the necessary expense test.
For monetary gifts of more than $2 million, authority rests with the CDC COO.
The timeframe may be extended in cases where the decision on the acceptance of a gift is difficult.
If it is determined that there are excess funds.
Is the donor an acceptable source?
Does the gift meet generally acceptable public standards?
Is the gift reasonable to administer?
Will the principle beneficiary of the gift be CDC?
Is there an actual or apparent conflict of interest?
Does CDC have an interest in accepting the gift?
Will accepting the gift reflect unfavorably on the ability of CDC or any employee to carry out responsibilities or official duties in a fair and objective manner?
Has the Office of the General Counsel been consulted to provide guidance on problematic issues about the acceptance of the gift?

constraints.text: The CDC does not accept gifts if the conditions imposed by the donor are illegal, contrary to public policy, unreasonable to administer, contrary to generally accepted public standards, an actual conflict of interest, or an apparent conflict of interest to a reasonable person.

CDC will not accept a gift that is conditioned upon any expenditure that cannot be covered by the gift itself or from income earned from the gift, unless an Act of Congress has approved such expenditure.
Offers of property will not be accepted if the total costs associated with acceptance are expected to exceed the cost of purchasing a similar item and the cost of normal care and maintenance.
Gifts may not be accepted if the conditions placed on the gift create a conflict of interest.
